content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are having a tea party!

Of course, there must be snacks at a tea party.

Laid out on the table in front of Alice and Bob are biscuits and cakes.

Initially, there are XXX biscuits and YYY cakes.

The two take turns eating snacks. Alice goes first.

On their turn, a person will do **exactly one** of the following three:


Eat one biscuit.

This reduces XXX by 111, and doesn't change YYY.


Eat one biscuit and one cake.

This reduces both XXX and YYY by 111.


Eat one cake, and then add one more biscuit to the table.

This reduces YYY by 111, and increases XXX by 111.


The number of cakes and biscuits on the table must remain non-negative at any point of time - so it's not allowed to eat a biscuit if there are no remaining biscuits, for example.

The person who eats the last snack will be satisfied.

Both Alice and Bob will choose their moves optimally in an attempt to be satisfied.

Given the initial values of XXX and YYY, can you decide who will be satisfied in the end?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single line of input, containing two space-separated integers XXX and YYY — the initial number of biscuits and cakes, respectively.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: either Alice\tt{Alice}Alice or Bob\tt{Bob}Bob, depending on who will be satisfied under optimal snack-eating choices.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, for example the strings bob,BOB,bOB\tt{bob}, \tt{BOB}, \tt{bOB}bob,BOB,bOB will all be accepted if Bob is the winner.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: There are no cakes, so both Alice and Bob are forced to always eat one biscuit on their move.
There are X=2X = 2X=2 biscuits, so Bob eats the last one because Alice starts.
Test case 222: There are still no cakes, and so once again Alice's and Bob's moves are forced.
This time there are X=3X = 3X=3 biscuits, so Alice will eat the last one.
Test case 333: There's one cake but no biscuits.

On her turn, Alice is forced to do a type-333 move, i.e. eat one cake and then place one biscuit on the table.
On Bob's turn, there's one biscuit so he just eats it. This is the last snack remaining, so Bob wins.

Test case 444: Initially, the table has 111 biscuit and 222 cakes.

On the first move, Alice can perform a type-222 move, i.e. eat both one biscuit and one cake.
On Bob's turn, the table now has zero biscuits and one cake available.
This is the same as the third sample case, just that Bob has to make a move now - so he loses and Alice wins.
